protocol: medical - supraventricular tachycardia (including atrial fibrillation) medical - tachycardia medical - ventricular tachycardia with a pulse (protocol 2 - 8)

dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for Transfer/Interfacility/Palliative Care call at a surgical center. Patient experiencing A-fib.
Hi, I'm Mark, an EMS provider with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
The patient needs to be transferred to the hospital for evaluation of atrial fibrillation. She was here for a port placement.
And how long has she been experiencing the A-fib?
We are not exactly sure. She was here for a port placement for chemotherapy. The chart notes A-fib started around 1100 this morning.
Can you tell me your name and where you are right now, ma'am?
My name is Barbara, and I'm in the surgical center.
Can you tell me what county we're in?
Henrico.
Can you squeeze my fingers right now, Barbara?
Sure.
And today is what date?
June 8th.
Great job. And can you tell me the name of this hospital or facility?
Surgical center, I think.
You're doing great, Barbara. Thank you.
We are going to take a few questions to get your history. Can you tell me your age?
I'm 72 years old.
What symptoms or problems have you noticed since this morning? And have you asked anyone at the facility if something was wrong?

I came in for the port placement, and they said I had a lower blood sugar. I was treated with some glucose. After that I had no symptoms. The staff called 911 because of my heart rhythm. I don't feel sick.
Can you share with us your medical history? Any past illnesses that are relevant?
I have pancreatic cancer, diabetes, and arthritis. I also suffer from high blood pressure.
What medications do you typically take?
I take insulin, metformin, carvedilol, amlodipine, aspirin, and ferrous sulfate.
Do you have any known drug allergies?
No known drug allergies.
Was there a particular event that caused this to happen? Like exercise, emotional stress, or something else?
I was having the port put in. I don't think I was active or stressed.
When was the last time you ate or drank?
Yesterday.
What were you doing when the staff first noticed something was wrong?
I was lying down in the operating room. They said I had a change in heart rhythm. I don't feel it now.
Got it. Thank you, Barbara.
On a scale of 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst pain you can imagine, how would you describe any pain or discomfort you're experiencing right now?
I don't have any pain.
Got it. Any symptoms of coughing, shortness of breath, fever, or nausea?
No, I feel fine.
Can we do a few checks to assess how you're doing medically?
Sure, I don't mind.
Do you have any trouble breathing at all, or is your throat feeling congested?
I've had no difficulty breathing.
Clear airway noted. No signs of obstruction observed.
Good, everything looks clear.
Can you take a deep breath for me? Let me check your lungs.
Lungs are clear bilaterally. Equal chest rise and fall.
Respiratory rate is 18. Oxygen saturation is 99% on room air.
You're breathing fine. I'll just keep a close watch on you.
Let's check your pulse and skin.
Radial pulse is present and strong. Heart rate is 80 BPM.
Skin is pink, warm, and dry.
There is grade 1 peripheral edema in the ankles. Capillary nail bed refill is 2-4 seconds.
Lungs remain clear. Our monitor shows she is in atrial fibrillation with occasional PVCs. No ST segment changes.
Blood pressure is 152/80 mmHg.
Your abdomen is soft and non-tender.
There is a surgical port present. No guarding or rigidity.
Blood pressure is 152/80 mmHg.
Respirations are 18.
Pulse is 80 BPM and regular.
Blood glucose is 103 mg/dL.
Oxygen saturation is 99% on room air.
Atrial fibrillation with occasional PVCs. A 12-lead ECG was completed and stored.
The patient reports no pain or discomfort at this time.
She is alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation-GCS 15.
A 22 gauge IV is already in place at the facility. The patient is currently on a cardiac monitor in 4-lead monitoring mode.
The facility staff gave her D50 IM per standing order to treat a previous low blood sugar of 55 mg/dL. That improved her initial sugar to 103.
The patient's temperature is 98.6°F.
Oxygen saturation is now 100% on room air.
We are following the established protocols for atrial fibrillation. Transport to the hospital is recommended for continued observation and management.
Let me check vitals again to make sure we're consistent.
Pulse is 75.
Breathing is still at 18.
Blood pressure is 174/47 mmHg.
Blood glucose is currently 0 mg/dL.
Oxygen saturation is 100% on room air.
Barbara, let me confirm your medications. You're taking insulin, metformin, carvedilol, amlodipine, and more. You were also treated for low blood sugar earlier.
Yes, they gave me D50 and started me on D5W. I don't feel faint or shaky now.
The facility has her on IV maintenance and wants her transported to VCU for monitoring. Are you okay with going there?
Yes, my doctor told me to go with you.
Barbara, you mentioned you can scoot over to the stretcher. It might be a little uncomfortable, but if you're okay with it, we can assist along with you.
Yes, I can manage. Thank you for letting me help out.
We'll help you onto the stretcher. I'll hold the sides to make it steady.
There you go. Secure on the stretcher now. Partner, let's lock the wheels and go over the straps.
All straps are locked. She's ready for loading into the ambulance.
We just need verbal consent for treatment and transport to the hospital. Are you okay with that, Barbara?
Yes, I am. I understand what we're doing and the care you're giving.
We're preparing for transport now. Can you stay with us a bit longer while we make sure you're secure and comfortable?
Of course, I'll be right here.

On arrival at the hospital, the patient had stable vitals with ongoing atrial fibrillation. A 12-lead ECG was performed, showing no ST segment changes. Her current BG is 0. She is on a D5W IV and a cardiac monitor.
She is asymptomatic on arrival. IV is stable and running as before, with facility staff having administered D50 to resolve hypoglycemia.
Per her own report, she had a new port placed recently for chemotherapy due to pancreatic cancer. She also has a history of hypertension, diabetes, and osteoarthritis.
We used the stretcher transfer method. Upon arrival at the hospital she was ambulated from the stretcher to her assigned bed without assistance.
She signed consents for treatment, transport, and billing. ER staff can take over now.
Thanks for the thorough report. Her chart from the facility is here. You're clear to return to service.
No further action is needed. Best of luck to her. Have a great shift!
Thanks, have a great one too!
